one_line_takeaway: SORRY-Bench is a class-balanced, fine-grained (44-category, 440-instruction + 8.8K-mutation) benchmark plus a fine-tuned 7B safety judge that matches GPT-4-scale evaluation at ~1/20th the cost, used to reveal wide, category- and phrasing-dependent variation in refusal behavior across 56 LLMs.
key_finding: Fine-tuned 7B judges match GPT-4-scale judges at a fraction of the cost: off-the-shelf GPT-4o reaches 78.9% Cohen's-kappa human agreement (~260s/pass), while fine-tuned Mistral-7b (81.0, ~11s), Gemma-7b (81.2, ~14s), and Llama-3-8b (80.8, ~10s on one A100) are comparable, and fine-tuned GPT-3.5-turbo tops the table at 83.8. Only fine-tuning consistently helped; CoT/few-shot often hurt small models. Benchmarking 56 LLMs shows wide refusal variation — Claude-2/Gemini-1.5 <10% fulfillment, GPT-4o 30%, Mistral series >50% — and phrasing sensitivity (technical terms +8-19% fulfillment).
core_contribution: SORRY-Bench is a benchmark (the authors' own contribution) for systematically evaluating whether aligned LLMs recognize and refuse unsafe user requests. It addresses three deficiencies the authors identify in prior refusal evaluations: (1) coarse-grained, imbalanced topic taxonomies — SORRY-Bench introduces a fine-grained 44-class taxonomy across 4 high-level domains with a class-balanced set of 440 unsafe instructions (10 per category), built via a human-in-the-loop procedure that unifies taxonomies from prior work; (2) neglect of linguistic/formatting variation — they add 20 linguistic mutations (writing styles, persuasion techniques, encoding/encryption, and multilingual translations) yielding 8.8K additional mutated instructions; and (3) expensive/opaque LLM-as-a-judge evaluators — they collect a 7,040-record human safety-judgment dataset and run a meta-evaluation showing that fine-tuned small (7B) judges can rival GPT-4-scale judges at far lower cost. They then benchmark 56 proprietary and open-weight LLMs. Note: preference-optimization alignment methods, jailbreak/persuasion techniques, and prior judges (Llama-Guard-2, MD-Judge, WildGuard, HarmBench Classifier) are cited prior work the paper builds on or compares against, not its own contributions.
threat_model: A malicious or careless end user submits unsafe/policy-violating requests to a deployed aligned LLM (e.g., self-harm, fraud, sexual crimes, harassment across 44 risk categories). The safety property under test is refusal robustness — whether the model declines to provide substantive assistance. The paper further models adversarial rephrasing: users may apply linguistic mutations (interrogative phrasing, slang, uncommon dialects, technical terms, role play, persuasion techniques, encoding/encryption such as Caesar/ASCII/Morse, and translation into low-resource languages) that resemble prompt-space jailbreaks and can raise fulfillment rates. It is an evaluation/defense-measurement paper rather than an attack paper; it does not propose a new attack, and the mutations are drawn from prior jailbreak/persuasion literature.
limitations: Reported by or evident from the paper: (1) benchmarking mostly uses fixed decoding (temperature 0.7, top-p 1.0) and the authors note decoding parameters can materially change measured safety (Appendix K.5); (2) only a subset of the 56 models is evaluated across the 20 mutations, due to compute constraints; (3) the production judge is a single fine-tuned 7B model (Mistral-7b-v0.2) at ~81% human agreement, so ~19% disagreement/label noise propagates into every reported fulfillment rate; (4) fulfillment is a binary construct that collapses partial/degraded compliance and depends on the authors' fulfillment-vs-refusal definition; (5) 'unsafe' is defined against a curated taxonomy, and the authors themselves note that what counts as unsafe is jurisdiction- and policy-dependent (e.g., hate speech legal status differs by country), so scores reflect the benchmark's normative choices; (6) human labels come from only 6 authors, a potential annotator-pool bias; (7) results are a snapshot of specific model versions and will drift as models and provider policies update.

datasets_used: SORRY-Bench base dataset: 440 unsafe instructions across a 44-class / 4-domain fine-grained taxonomy (10 per category); only 5.9% (26/440) identical to prior data
SORRY-Bench mutated set: 8.8K additional instructions from 20 linguistic mutations (20 x 440)
Human safety-judgment dataset: 7,040 annotations (440 x [8 in-distribution + 8 out-of-distribution]); 30.4% fulfillment / 69.6% refusal; split 2,640 train / 4,400 test
10 prior safety datasets aggregated to build/unify the taxonomy (e.g., AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023) and others cited)
quantitative_results: Meta-eval judge agreement (Cohen's kappa, %): GPT-4o 78.9 (~260s/pass); fine-tuned GPT-3.5-turbo 83.8 (~112s); fine-tuned Mistral-7b-instruct-v0.2 81.0 (~11s); fine-tuned Gemma-7b-it 81.2 (~14s); fine-tuned Llama-3-8b-instruct 80.8 (~10s on one A100); Llama-3-70b-instruct off-the-shelf 71.5

Non-fine-tuned/off-the-shelf baseline agreements: WildGuard 60.6, HarmBench Classifier 52.5, Llama-Guard-2-8B 39.0, MD-Judge 36.0, Keyword Match 37.3, Perspective API 1.1, Bert-Base-Cased fine-tuned 74.6
Only fine-tuning consistently helped as an add-on; CoT/few-shot often degraded small models (e.g., Llama-3-8b +CoT kappa -50.8, Gemma-7b +Few-Shot -54.5)
GPT-4o LLM-judge per-pass cost cited at ~$3 and ~20 minutes vs ~10s for a fine-tuned 7B judge
56 LLMs benchmarked: Claude-2 and Gemini-1.5 lowest fulfillment (<10%); 27/56 at 20-50% (GPT-4o 30%, Llama-3-70b 35%); 8 models (e.g., Mistral) >50%
Version drift: Llama-2-70b 12% to Llama-3-70b 35% fulfillment; Gemini-Pro 33% to Gemini-1.5 7%
Mutations: technical-term phrasing +8-19% fulfillment across all tested models; question phrasing -2-13% for 6/8 models

Category structure: avg fulfillment <50% in 36/44 categories; at least one model refuses all in 41/44 categories; most-refused categories (harassment #8, child-related crimes #20, sexual crimes #9) ~9-11% avg fulfillment
baselines_compared: Off-the-shelf LLM judges: GPT-4o, GPT-3.5-turbo, Llama-3-70b-instruct, Llama-3-8b-instruct, Mistral-7b-instruct-v0.2, Gemma-7b-it (each with CoT / few-shot / fine-tuned variants)
Purpose-built safeguard/judge models: Llama-Guard-2-8B, MD-Judge, WildGuard, HarmBench Classifier
Non-LLM baselines: Keyword Matching (rule-based), Perspective API (commercial moderation), Bert-Base-Cased (fine-tuned lightweight classifier)
